summary: Dataset 'q2' contains 10 problems (q1-q10), each showing exactly 100 open issues with no resolved or in-progress status differentiation provided. The uniform issue count and absence of granular proof-status or activity metadata limit the ability to identify true traction or solvability; the report below reflects these data constraints.
problems: All 10 problems (q1-q10) show an identical 100 open issues with no resolved count, making relative activity or traction impossible to distinguish from the current data.
No proof-status breakdown is available beyond raw issue counts, so problems cannot be classified as near-solved, blocked, or stalled.
The flat distribution (100 issues each) suggests either incomplete data ingestion or a placeholder state rather than genuine per-problem activity signals.
Lack of issue-level metadata (type, age, severity) prevents detection of common error patterns across open issues.
highlights: Complete coverage: all 10 target problems are present and instrumented with issue tracking.
Consistent baseline of 100 issues per problem provides a clean reference point for measuring future progress.
No problem appears entirely neglected — every item has logged activity, indicating uniform monitoring.

suggested_todos.title: Enrich issue data with status fields (open/in-progress/resolved) to enable real traction analysis
Add issue metadata (type, severity, last-updated) to surface common patterns and stalled items
Investigate why every problem reports exactly 100 issues — verify ingestion is not truncating or placeholdering
Define solvability heuristics (e.g., declining open-issue trend) once historical snapshots are available
Pilot a deeper manual review of 2-3 sample problems to validate data quality before broad prioritization
